1: νεκρούς (dead) is the controlling metaphor of Ephesians 2:1-10: spiritual death as present condition, not future threat. The dative case τοῖς παραπτώμασιν καὶ ταῖς ἁμαρτίαις (in/by trespasses and sins) is instrumental or locative — the sphere or the cause of death. Paul uses both words: παράπτωμα (a sideways fall/trespass — deliberate deviation) and ἁμαρτία (missing the mark — the broader category). Together they cover the range of human moral failure.
2: κατὰ τὸν αἰῶνα τοῦ κόσμου τούτου (according to the age of this world) — two terms for the present fallen order: aiōn (age, temporal frame) and kosmos (world, ordered system). ἄρχοντα τῆς ἐξουσίας τοῦ ἀέρος (ruler of the authority of the air) — the aerial domain in first-century cosmology was associated with malevolent spiritual forces. τοῦ πνεύματος τοῦ νῦν ἐνεργοῦντος (the spirit now working — present participle) marks present-tense activity, not merely past influence.
3: σάρξ (G4561, dispute_level=4) here means the creaturely human nature in its misdirected inclination — not the physical body as evil in itself, but the whole person oriented away from God. τὰ θελήματα τῆς σαρκός (the desires of the flesh) and τῶν διανοιῶν (of the mind) pair physical impulse with intellectual disposition — both need redemption. τέκνα φύσει ὀργῆς (children by nature of wrath) — phusei (by nature) indicates the condition is not merely acquired but constitutional.
4: ὁ δὲ θεός (but God) — the adversative marks the theological turning point of the passage. The divine character is specified before the action: πλούσιος ὢν ἐν ἐλέει (being rich in mercy — present participle, describing God's ongoing character) and διὰ τὴν πολλὴν ἀγάπην αὐτοῦ (because of his great love). The reversal of Ephesians 2:1-3 flows not from human reformation but from God's own nature — mercy and love are the sources, not human deserving.
5: συνεζωοποίησεν (made alive together with — aorist, compound with syn-) is the first of three syn- compound verbs in Ephesians 2:5-6 expressing corporate union with Christ. The parenthetical χάριτί ἐστε σεσωσμένοι (by grace you are saved — perfect passive periphrastic) disrupts the syntax to insert the theological foundation: salvation as a present state resulting from a past completed act. ἐστε (you are) in the present; σεσωσμένοι (having been saved) as the standing result.
6: συνήγειρεν (raised together — aorist) and συνεκάθισεν (seated together — aorist) use the same syn- prefix: believers are co-raised and co-seated with Christ. The session of believers ἐν τοῖς ἐπουρανίοις (in the heavenly realms) is presently true — not merely future. The aorist tenses view the action as complete; the present reality is the consequence. This is a realized participation, not merely a promised location.
7: ἐν τοῖς αἰῶσιν τοῖς ἐπερχομένοις (in the coming ages — present participle, ongoing sequence) extends the demonstration of grace into the future. ὑπερβάλλον πλοῦτος τῆς χάριτος (surpassing riches of grace) — hyperballon appears again (cf. Ephesians 1:19); the excess of grace exceeds calculation. χρηστότης (kindness) specifies the character of grace — not stern magnanimity but warm, active good-will toward the undeserving.
8: τῇ χάριτί ἐστε σεσωσμένοι διὰ πίστεως — the perfect periphrastic restated from Ephesians 2:5. The instrument is πίστεως (faith — dative of instrument), but the critical point: τοῦτο οὐκ ἐξ ὑμῶν (this not from yourselves) — touto is neuter, while pistis (faith) is feminine. The pronoun therefore cannot refer to faith alone; it points to the entire salvation described in Ephesians 2:5-8. θεοῦ τὸ δῶρον (the gift of God — emphatic position) — salvation as the whole is God's gift; faith is the means, not the meritorious ground.
9: οὐκ ἐξ ἔργων (not from works — emphatic negation) eliminates human achievement as contributing cause. ἵνα μή τις καυχήσηται (so that no one might boast — purpose clause with subjunctive) states the design: the exclusion of boasting is a divine goal, not incidental. The construction is purpose, not mere result — God structured salvation to exclude the condition that would generate human pride.
10: ποίημα (poiēma, workmanship/work of art) — the word carries the connotation of a crafted object; English "poem" derives from it. God is the craftsman; believers are his creation (second creation in Christ). κτισθέντες (created — aorist passive; decisively recreated). ἐπὶ ἔργοις ἀγαθοῖς (for good works — purposive) — good works are the purpose of the new creation, not the basis. προητοίμασεν (prepared in advance — aorist; God pre-arranged the works) — they walk in a path already laid.
11: ἀκροβυστία (uncircumcision) and περιτομή (circumcision) — the defining social-religious boundary of the first century. The qualifier χειροποιήτου (made by hands) is pointed: it reduces the circumcision to a human physical act, underscoring that the superiority it claimed was itself of human construction. Paul's audience is asked to remember this status as past, not as present identity.
12: Five descriptors of the Gentile condition apart from Christ: without Christ, excluded from citizenship in Israel, strangers to the covenants of promise, without hope, and ἄθεοι (atheoi, without God/atheists) in the world. The plural διαθηκῶν (covenants — not one covenant but multiple) points to the Abrahamic, Mosaic, Davidic, and new covenant promises collectively. To be a stranger to these is to have no access to their framing of reality.
13: νυνὶ δέ (but now — strong temporal contrast) marks the reversal. ἐν Χριστῷ Ἰησοῦ (in Christ Jesus — the sphere of the change) and ἐν τῷ αἵματι τοῦ Χριστοῦ (by the blood of Christ — the means). ἐγγύς (near) draws on Isaiah 57:19; those who were far have been brought into proximity — not geographical but covenantal. The spatial language (far/near) was standard for Gentile/Jew distinction in Second Temple literature.
14: αὐτὸς γάρ ἐστιν ἡ εἰρήνη ἡμῶν (for he himself is our peace — emphatic autos) — Christ does not merely make peace or announce it; he is the peace. μεσότοιχον τοῦ φραγμοῦ (middle wall of partition — compound noun, NT hapax mesotoichon) — the dividing wall. Whether this alludes to the Temple soreg (barrier keeping Gentiles from the inner courts) or is purely metaphorical, the image is of a structural barrier demolished.
15: καταργήσας (having abolished/rendered inoperative — aorist participle) describes Christ's relation to τὸν νόμον τῶν ἐντολῶν ἐν δόγμασιν (the law of commandments in ordinances). The focus is on the divisive legal code that enforced the Jew/Gentile boundary, not the entire Mosaic Torah as moral category. ἕνα καινὸν ἄνθρωπον (one new humanity/person) — kainos (new in quality, not just temporal novelty) and anthrōpos (human, not anēr/male) — a new corporate humanity created from two.
16: ἀποκτείνας τὴν ἔχθραν ἐν αὐτῷ — the enmity is killed in/by him (Christ) or in/by it (the cross). The ambiguity of autō may be productive: Christ on the cross kills the enmity by bearing it. ἀποκαταλλάξῃ (reconcile — compound verb with apo- indicating complete or restored reconciliation) — both groups in one body to God. The cross achieves vertical reconciliation (to God) through horizontal unification (Jew and Gentile).
17: ἐλθὼν εὐηγγελίσατο εἰρήνην (coming he proclaimed peace — aorist; a completed proclamation) echoes Isaiah 57:19. The two groups are addressed in reverse order from what might be expected: ὑμῖν τοῖς μακράν (to you who were far — the Gentiles addressed first) and τοῖς ἐγγύς (to those who were near — Israel). The same Christ preaches peace to both; the message is one.
18: προσαγωγή (prosagōgē, access) is a technical term in the ancient world for admission into the presence of royalty or deity — the right of approach. δι᾽ αὐτοῦ (through him — the mediatorial route is Christ) and ἐν ἑνὶ πνεύματι (in one Spirit — the sphere of approach) make the Trinitarian structure explicit: to the Father, through the Son, in the Spirit. Both groups — ἀμφότεροι (both) — share one access, not two separate routes.
19: οὐκέτι ἐστὲ ξένοι καὶ πάροικοι (no longer strangers and resident aliens — legal resident alien status without citizenship rights). The contrast terms are συμπολῖται τῶν ἁγίων (fellow-citizens with the saints — full civic membership) and οἰκεῖοι τοῦ θεοῦ (members of God's household — intimate kinship, not merely civic). The movement is from legal alien to citizen to family member — escalating in relational depth.
20: ἐποικοδομηθέντες (having been built upon — aorist passive) continues the architectural metaphor. θεμέλιον τῶν ἀποστόλων καὶ προφητῶν (foundation of the apostles and prophets) — the genitive is appositional: the apostles and prophets are the foundation (not its layers). ἀκρογωνιαῖος (akrogōniaios, cornerstone) — debated as foundation cornerstone (binding two walls at the base) or capstone (binding at the top); the building imagery here favors the former: Christ as the determinative stone from which all else is aligned.
21: πᾶσα οἰκοδομή — without the article, this may mean "every building" (each local community) rather than "the whole building" (one universal structure). The present participle αὔξει (is growing) makes the temple a living, ongoing construction — not a completed monument but a dynamic growth. εἰς ναὸν ἅγιον (into a holy temple) — naos is the inner sanctuary, not the outer courts (hieron); the whole community is growing into the most sacred space.
22: συνοικοδομεῖσθε (you are being built together — present passive) adds the syn- prefix (together) and addresses the Ephesian community specifically. The ongoing present passive indicates the process is incomplete and God is the builder. κατοικητήριον τοῦ θεοῦ ἐν πνεύματι (dwelling place of God in/by the Spirit) — katoikētērion is permanent dwelling (contrast paroikia, sojourning); the Spirit is either the sphere or the agent of this indwelling.
